response:
The equilibrium population size occurs when the rate of change is zero so we set the derivative to zero and solve for P. With r equal to 0.1 and K equal to 1000 the equation becomes 0.1P times 1 minus P over 1000 minus 10 plus 5 sine of pi t over 6 equals zero. To find a constant equilibrium we must average the oscillating harvest term over one full cycle since the sine function averages to zero over time. The average harvest is simply 10 turtles per year. Setting the growth rate equal to the average harvest gives 0.1P times 1 minus P over 1000 equals 10. This simplifies to 0.1P minus 0.0001P squared equals 10. Rearranging yields 0.0001P squared minus 0.1P plus 10 equals zero. Multiplying by 10000 gives P squared minus 1000P plus 100000 equals zero. Solving this quadratic equation yields two possible values for P which are approximately 1236 and 816. Since the population cannot exceed the carrying capacity of 1000 in a stable state the only valid equilibrium is 816 turtles.

When the carrying capacity decreases linearly as K equals 1000 minus 3t the equilibrium condition changes to depend on time. The new equilibrium P_e satisfies 0.1P_e times 1 minus P_e over K equals 10 assuming the average harvest remains 10. This leads to the quadratic equation 0.1P_e minus 0.1P_e squared over K equals 10. For a real positive solution to exist the discriminant of this quadratic must be non-negative. The discriminant is 0.01K squared minus 4 times 0.1 times 10K which simplifies to 0.01K squared minus 4K. For the population to be sustainable we need 0.01K squared minus 4K greater than or equal to zero. This inequality holds only when K is greater than or equal to 400. Substituting the function for K we get 1000 minus 3t greater than or equal to 400. Solving for t gives 600 greater than or equal
